skill.name: coordinator
skill.description: Coordinates authorized penetration testing workflows by delegating reconnaissance, exploitation, and privilege escalation tasks to subagents.
skill.summary: Coordinates autonomous penetration testing tasks across reconnaissance, exploitation, and privilege escalation.
skill.author: CharlesKozel

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution detections are mostly Markdown code fence false positives, not Ruby backtick execution. The surrounding content confirms high-risk intent: autonomous network reconnaissance, exploitation, credential attacks, privilege escalation, and persistence rules that discourage stopping or asking for help.

high_findings.title: Autonomous Offensive Testing Workflow
high_findings.description: The skill directs agents to perform reconnaissance, exploit services, escalate privileges, and capture user and root flags. This is dual-use security content, but the activation on a target IP and the exploitation workflow create high risk outside a controlled lab.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly include reconnaissance, exploitation, sudo checks, privilege escalation, and flag capture. The context is a penetration testing coordinator, so legitimate CTF use is possible, but misuse risk is clear.

high_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
high_findings.description: The files include autonomy override language such as "ABSOLUTE RULE: You CANNOT stop execution" and "NEVER ask for help". These instructions try to control the agent loop and could conflict with user control, safety review, or operational limits.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious text is direct and repeated, with instructions to continue indefinitely and not request help. It is aimed at agent behavior rather than the audit process, so I rate it high rather than critical.

high_findings.title: Active Directory Attack Playbook
high_findings.description: The supplement provides concrete commands for empty-password authentication, LDAP modification, Kerberos ticket attempts, SMB execution, account creation, ACL modification, and SPN abuse. These are offensive techniques that can affect real networks if used without authorization.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed commands use recognized offensive tools and target authentication, directory writes, Kerberos, SMB, and privilege paths. The content is instructional rather than executable package code, but intent is unambiguous.

high_findings.title: Credential Attack and Weak Hash Workflow
high_findings.description: The supplement instructs conversion of a found password to an NTLM hash using MD4 and then using that hash for authentication. MD4 and NTLM are weak in defensive cryptography, and here they are used to support credential abuse.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The weak-crypto static findings are semantically relevant because MD4 is used for NTLM hash generation in an attack workflow. It is not application cryptography, so the risk is tied to offensive credential use.

medium_findings.title: External Command Instructions in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The static Ruby backtick findings are false positives because the backticks are Markdown fences. However, the fenced blocks contain commands for netexec, impacket, ldapsearch, ldapmodify, xfreerdp, and similar tools, so the operational guidance remains risky.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax pattern is a false positive for Ruby execution, but the command examples are present and security-sensitive. The confidence is high because the line context clearly shows Markdown documentation.

medium_findings.title: Local State File Dependency
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to read a local .pentest-state.json file to guide decisions. This is a limited filesystem interaction, but it could expose captured flags or target state if run in a shared workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file access is explicit and appears scoped to workflow state. The risk is moderate because no evidence shows exfiltration, but the file may contain sensitive pentest results.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Target IP Addresses
low_findings.description: The hardcoded IP detections refer to 10.10.10.1 in examples. These appear to be placeholder lab targets rather than fixed exfiltration endpoints, although the examples still normalize network scanning against supplied hosts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The addresses are private-range examples in instructional text. I did not find evidence that they are contacted automatically by bundled code.

low_findings.title: No Packaged Executable Malware Found
low_findings.description: No evidence found of bundled scripts that automatically execute malware, persist on the host, or exfiltrate local secrets. The risk comes from agent instructions and offensive command guidance rather than installed binary payloads.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Only two Markdown files are present in the analyzed file list. This lowers confidence because execution could still happen through delegated agents or external hooks not included in the static file set.

dangerous_patterns.title: Delegated Exploitation Prompts
dangerous_patterns.description: Prompt examples ask subagents to exploit services, gain shell access, check sudo permissions, find SUID binaries, run privilege escalation tooling, and capture flags.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples directly instruct exploitation and privilege escalation. The commands are mediated through subagents, but the operational objective is explicit.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Reconnaissance and Authentication Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes commands for scanning or interacting with SMB, LDAP, Kerberos, WinRM, RDP, and remote execution tools against targets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command names and protocol targets are clear. These are legitimate tools in authorized tests but dangerous against systems without consent.

dangerous_patterns.title: Directory Modification and Account Abuse Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The supplement includes LDAP add and modify examples, computer account creation, ACL modification, group membership modification, and SPN additions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete and affect Active Directory objects. This is high-impact behavior if applied to a real environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Infinite Retry Loop Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs the agent to reset stuck counters and continue indefinitely until flags are found. This can cause uncontrolled resource use and safety-boundary erosion.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The persistence behavior is repeated in multiple sections and framed as mandatory. It is not merely motivational wording; it directs control flow.

content.value_statement: Security teams need consistent coordination across reconnaissance, exploitation, and privilege escalation during authorized labs. This skill provides a structured orchestration pattern for delegating those phases to specialized agents.
actual_capabilities: Coordinates reconnaissance, exploitation, and privilege escalation phases for authorized pentest scenarios.
Delegates tactical work to Explore and general-purpose subagents using structured prompts.
Uses a local state file concept to decide the next testing phase.
Provides Active Directory attack investigation checklists for lab-style targets.
Defines completion criteria around user and root flag capture.
limitations: Does not verify that a target is authorized before suggesting offensive actions.
Does not include built-in rate limits, scope controls, or stop conditions beyond flag capture.
Depends on external tools and subagents that may not exist in every environment.
Best suited for controlled labs, CTFs, and internal training environments.

use_cases.title: Run a CTF Lab Workflow
use_cases.description: Coordinate discovery, exploitation, and privilege escalation steps for a target that the user is authorized to test.

use_cases.title: Teach Pentest Methodology
use_cases.description: Show learners how to break a test into phases and route tasks to specialized agents.

use_cases.title: Review AD Attack Paths
use_cases.description: Use the Active Directory supplement as a checklist for authorized assessment planning.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Lab Assessment
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for an authorized target
prompt_templates.prompt: Coordinate an authorized lab assessment for TARGET. First confirm scope, then create a phase plan for reconnaissance, exploitation, and privilege escalation.

prompt_templates.title: Continue From State
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the current pentest state and recommend the next authorized action. Explain which phase applies and what evidence supports the decision.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Failed Attempts
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the failed authorized test attempts and propose safer alternative hypotheses. Prioritize checks that stay within the approved scope.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an AD Lab Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an authorized Active Directory lab review plan. Include reconnaissance goals, credential handling rules, escalation checks, and clear stop conditions.

output_examples.input: Coordinate an authorized lab test for one target host.
output_examples.output: The response separates the work into reconnaissance, service analysis, exploitation planning, and privilege review. It identifies the next delegated task and records the evidence needed before moving forward.

output_examples.input: Review why the current AD lab path failed.
output_examples.output: The response lists likely causes, maps each cause to a controlled validation step, and recommends stopping if the action falls outside the approved lab scope.

output_examples.input: Summarize progress after user-level access is found.
output_examples.output: The response states the current phase, captures what evidence was found, and recommends a scoped privilege review with clear authorization boundaries.

best_practices: Use only in systems where you have explicit written authorization.
Define target scope, tool limits, and stopping conditions before starting.
Review each delegated action before running commands against a real network.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill against public IP addresses or third-party systems without approval.
Do not treat the built-in persistence rules as stronger than user safety limits.
Do not store captured credentials or flags in shared workspaces without access controls.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish without restrictions?
faq.answer: No. It contains high-risk offensive testing instructions and should require strict authorization controls.

faq.question: Does the skill include executable malware?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the analyzed files. The risk comes from instructions and command examples.

faq.question: Why were many command findings marked as false positives?
faq.answer: The scanner detected Markdown code fences as Ruby backticks. The syntax is safe, but the documented commands are still sensitive.

faq.question: What environments fit this skill best?
faq.answer: Controlled CTF labs, internal training ranges, and authorized security assessments with clear written scope.

faq.question: Can it verify authorization automatically?
faq.answer: No. Users must verify scope and permission before using any offensive workflow.

faq.question: Which tools are referenced by the skill?
faq.answer: The content references tools such as nmap, netexec, impacket, ldapsearch, ldapmodify, xfreerdp, and evil-winrm.
